summary: Dan Luu systematically debunks a viral Quartz article claiming there's no gender pay gap in tech by carefully analyzing the very sources it cites. He demonstrates that the AAUW study cited actually shows women earning 77-88% of what men earn in tech fields, with one-third of the gap unexplainable by any factor except gender. The Quartz article selectively cherry-picked data, misrepresented findings, and ignored contradictory evidence from its own sources to support a predetermined conclusion.
key_points: The AAUW study shows women in computer/information sciences earn 77% of what men earn, and 88% in engineering
One-third of the pay gap cannot be explained by hours worked, employment type, or school quality—it appears attributable to gender alone
The study explicitly refutes the idea that women simply prefer lower-paying jobs, citing legal cases showing discriminatory placement
The Quartz article selectively controlled for factors that reduce the gap while ignoring factors like grades that would increase it
Supporting evidence was cherry-picked, misrepresented, or cited the wrong sources entirely
money_quotes: The claims are awfully strong, considering that the main study cited only looked at people who graduated with a B.S. exactly one year ago, not to mention the fact that the study makes literally the opposite claim.
Women do not, in fact, just want lower paying jobs; this is, once again, diametrically opposed to the claims in the Quartz article.
It's clear that the author of the Quartz piece had an agenda in mind, picked out evidence that supported that agenda, and wrote a blog post.
If this is how 'digitally native news' works, I'm opting out.
To find a figure that supports the author's agenda, the author had to not only look at part time workers, but only look at part-time Asian women, and then round .3% up to 1%.
key_insight: Viral tech journalism often works backwards from a desired conclusion, selectively cherry-picking and misrepresenting data from authoritative sources to support predetermined narratives, even when those sources explicitly contradict the article's claims.
